Iso for tail vein cannulation was stopped at 2:01 pm, >=1 hr before the start of the baseline recording. No clave; used luer-lock cap. Dexmedetomidine dilution was made by putting 0.75 mL of 0.1 mg/mL dexmedetomidine in 14.25 mL saline. There was a puddle under the rat by the time the infusion stopped. The rat was quite unconscious, so I assume it was urine, and not dexmedetomidine.

Iso for tail vein cannulation was stopped at 2:30 pm, >=1 hr before the start of the baseline recording. No clave; used luer-lock cap. Dexmedetomidine dilution was made by putting 0.3 mL of 0.1 mg/mL dexmedetomidine in 14.7 mL saline. Rat got cap off of catheter. I noticed when I went to connect the infusion tubing, so there was a little more air where the tubing connected than normal. The rat also took a long time to settle down after the infusion started. When I tried to move her to her side to test righting reflex, she did not let me and walked around, chewed on the tape on her tail, etc. Then she would go in and out of periods of stillness, like other rats do once they recover from dexmedetomidine. So, there was no return of righting reflex, as there was no known loss of it.

I did not know what to do, so I tried stopping the miniscope recording a few seconds later. I accidentally pressed the start recording button, it complained that it was already recording in the notes, and then I pressed the stop recording button. What a disaster.

Recording with 1 ms LED flash (Analog channel 1), at the same time as the MCS stimulator's sync out sends a TTL to the Neuralynx system (Sync 1) and turns on the miniscope recording (Sync 2). See miniscope_recording_timing_led.stm, test_miniscope_led_timing.cfg, and no_rat_UserConfig_V4_BNO_Miniscope_surgery.json (the experiment name is test_miniscope_led_timing). This is the first experiment after I upgraded the RAM in the recording laptop from 8 GB to 32 GB.

Iso for tail vein cannulation was stopped at 2:02 pm, >=1 hr before the start of the baseline recording. No clave; used luer-lock cap. The syringe pump wasn't clicked into place when the pumping started. It clicked into place a few seconds later, but I don't know whether it cause propofol to withdraw or come out more. (I tested it several times after the experiment and got both cases, but withdrawing was more common.) Always make sure the pump is clicked into place before starting it!

Iso for tail vein cannulation was stopped at 1:55 pm, >=1 hr before the start of the baseline recording. No clave; used luer-lock cap. Ketamine dilution was made by putting 0.9 mL of 100 mg/mL ketamine in 14.1 mL saline. Rat was pretty calm, even lethargic. Then, he seemed to have a seizure (or some sort of spastic movement) right as the infusion started. Lab members were in the room with me for the beginning of the baseline, then out, then back in for the infusion, then back out once the rat was under. There was talking and door opening/shutting and I was standing over the rat more than normal, but that was it.

Iso for tail vein cannulation was stopped at 1:45 pm, >=1 hr before the start of the baseline recording. No clave; used luer-lock cap. Dexmedetomidine dilution was made by putting 0.2 mL of 0.1 mg/mL dexmedetomidine in 9.8 mL saline. The omnetics connector wasn't plugging in very well, possibly due to a poor clip reattachment that I did when I cleaned him up after his first experiment. I eventually got it. After the experiment, I put him under isoflurane, drilled off the clip, and epoxied a new one on. It took a couple of rounds of isoflurane and epoxying (after I took him upstairs the first time, he bent the clip). I finally brought him back upstairs for the night around 9-9:30 pm.

I gave him a 10 mL saline injection after the experiment was over to hopefully help him recover from whatever sickness is causing this.

Recording with 1 ms LED flash (Analog channel 1) after 1 s, at the same time as the MCS stimulator's sync out sends a TTL to the Neuralynx system (Sync 1). The miniscope recording is turned on by Sync 2 for 2 s, and Sync 1 also gives 1 ms pulses that mark the time of the recording starting and stopping, with the beginning of the 1 ms pulse being the marker. See miniscope_recording_timing_led_middle.stm, test_miniscope_led_timing.cfg, and no_rat_UserConfig_V4_BNO_Miniscope_surgery.json (the experiment name is test_miniscope_led_timing).

There were some noises due to talking/etc. in our office, whispering between Ethan and me in the recording room, and doors opening/closing, including when I checked on the rat, but mostly it was quiet. Some packing materials were used to dampen the office door noise, and signs were placed on the door, which led to very little noise from the office. When I stopped the recording, the rat was still quite out of it, despite having righted himself earlier.

Just checking to see if this rat's image had improved from last time. I still could not find any active neurons. The ephys data looked good still, but it was not collected simultaneously with the calcium imaging. I took sample recordings at 30 Hz and 10 Hz, just for my records. I think I should give it another shot closer to when we are ready to do the immunohistochemistry--record a sample to see if there is discernable activity before I put in the tail IV, and then sacrifice the rat if there is not anything. I did not notice the rat being sick this time, but he was still very compliant. The rat was not weighed before the recordings but was weighed about 15 mins after the recordings.

Time at start of experiment is in the Neuralynx events file. There was stuff in the set screw that prevented us from doing a sleep experiment today, but we put him under iso to clean it out. I don't know when the iso was turned off relative to the start of the recording. The miniscope recording crashed a couple of times before this successful recording, and the second time it crashed I turned the Neuralynx box off without unplugging the rat. I then unplugged the rat, turned the box back on, and plugged the rat back in (I think in that order). The signals looked good still. When I asked, Christa said that they used to not turn the DAQ off with the rats plugged in out of an abundance of caution, in case there was a discharge, but that it wasn't supposed to happen.

Iso for tail vein cannulation was stopped at 1:49 pm, >=1 hr before the start of the baseline recording. No clave; used luer-lock cap. There were likely some noises due to talking/hallway noise/etc. and doors opening/closing, including when I checked on the rat, but mostly it was quiet. After the rat righted himself, horizontal bars started appearing on the miniscope image. I had seen them at least once prior to that, but they were not prevalent until after the rat was righted. By the end of the recording, the horizontal bars had lessened, but this may be an issue with analyses. The trash lady came by about 5:10 pm.

Time at start of experiment is in the Neuralynx events file. The rat was placed in the recording chamber at about 12:09 pm in order to give him a chance to habituate. Then, at around 1:09 pm, he was placed back in his home cage to await the actual recording (since there is no food or water in the recording chamber). There were some noises due to talking/etc. in our office and doors opening/closing, including when I checked on the rat, but mostly it was quiet. Like some of the previous experiments, there were masks over the door latch to help the main office door shut more quietly. The new Open Ephys miniscope also had horizontal bar noise that appeared, so I ended up switching to the DC jack for power. I then tried the Labmaker miniscope and the DC power seemed to fix the issue. The chip next to the coaxial cable connector on the miniscope PCB was quite hot (would be somewhat painful if you left your gloved finger on it for 20-30 seconds), but the horizontal bars were gone. So I used DC jack power (and changed the jumper position on the DAQ so it would pull power from the DC jack) and the Labmaker miniscope I used on all of the other experiments. Hopefully the heat isn't an issue. I spent so long handling the rat to swap out the different scopes/wires to troubleshoot things that I worry he was too agitated to sleep well, but we'll see with the data analysis.
